title: Comparative: Judicial Review
lead: Three models: India's ==constitutional supremacy + basic structure + PIL==; America's ==Marbury-style strong-form review==; Britain's ==parliamentary sovereignty with administrative legality review==.

head: Basis
India
United States
United Kingdom
rows: Written; ==Constitution supreme==; review protects rights and federalism.
==Uncodified; Parliament legally sovereign== — courts can't overrule primary Acts.
Not express — developed via [[Marbury v. Madison (1803)]]; Article III.
Common law, ==Human Rights Act 1998==, devolution statutes.
[[Kesavananda Bharati (1973)]] — review is ==part of basic structure==.
[[CCSU v. Minister for Civil Service (1985)]] — ==illegality, irrationality, procedural impropriety==.
SC/HCs can ==strike down laws== violating FRs or federal limits.
Courts ==cannot strike down Acts==; interpret compatibly where possible.
Once ratified under Article V, ==not substantively reviewable==.
No formal amendment process — ordinary legislation.
Strong — subject to ==standing, ripeness, political question==.
Struck down if ==ultra vires==, unconstitutional, excessive delegation.
Quashed if ultra vires or rights-incompatible.
Strong — Part III + ==substantive due process under Art 21==, privacy, dignity.
Weaker against Parliament; stronger against public authorities.
Limited — ==devolution competence== (Scotland Act: outside competence = ‘not law’).
Quashing/prohibiting/mandatory orders; ==JR and Courts Act 2022== adds suspended quashing orders.
==Very strong== — laws, executive action AND amendments.
Restraint, political question, presumption of constitutionality.
Standing, mootness, ==political question doctrine==, appointments ideology.
